biomarkers.name: Vitamin D (25-OH)
biomarkers.description: The primary circulating form of vitamin D; reflects total body stores from sun exposure, diet, and supplements.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: 2024 Endocrine Society: empiric supplementation recommended for at-risk groups (>75yo, pregnant, prediabetes). Routine screening NOT recommended for general population.

biomarkers.name: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin)
biomarkers.description: Essential for nerve function, DNA synthesis, and red blood cell formation. Confirm borderline cases with methylmalonic acid and homocysteine.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Symptoms can occur in 'low-normal' range. Methylmalonic acid & homocysteine help confirm borderline cases. Metformin use can lower B12.

biomarkers.name: Folate (Serum)
biomarkers.description: Critical for DNA synthesis, cell division, and methylation. Especially important during pregnancy.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: ALWAYS check B12 before supplementing folate to avoid masking B12 deficiency. MTHFR C677T variants may require methylfolate (5-MTHF).

biomarkers.name: Vitamin A (Retinol)
biomarkers.description: Essential for vision, immune function, and skin health. Deficiency rare in developed countries.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Toxicity risk with supplementation >10,000 IU/day. Beta-carotene (provitamin A) is safer for general use.

biomarkers.name: Ferritin
biomarkers.description: Primary storage form of iron. Best single test for iron deficiency, but falsely elevated during inflammation/infection.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Acute-phase reactant: can be falsely elevated with inflammation. Check CRP alongside. >500 ng/mL may indicate iron overload (hemochromatosis).

biomarkers.name: Serum Iron
biomarkers.description: Measures circulating iron bound to transferrin. Fluctuates widely throughout the day — best measured fasting, morning.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: High diurnal variation. Always interpret with ferritin, TIBC, and transferrin saturation for full picture.

biomarkers.name: TIBC (Total Iron Binding Capacity)
biomarkers.description: Measures the blood's capacity to bind iron with transferrin. Elevated in iron deficiency as body tries to capture more iron.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Elevated TIBC + low ferritin = iron deficiency. Low TIBC + high ferritin = iron overload or chronic disease.

biomarkers.name: Transferrin Saturation
biomarkers.description: Percentage of transferrin saturated with iron. Calculated: (Serum Iron / TIBC) × 100.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: <20% suggests iron deficiency. >45% may suggest iron overload (screen for hemochromatosis).

biomarkers.name: Magnesium (RBC)
biomarkers.description: Measures magnesium inside red blood cells — far more reliable indicator of intracellular/tissue stores than serum magnesium.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Gold standard for magnesium assessment. Serum Mg reflects only ~1% of body stores and has poor sensitivity. ~50% of Americans are Mg deficient.

biomarkers.name: Zinc (Serum)
biomarkers.description: Essential trace mineral for immune function, wound healing, DNA synthesis, and protein synthesis.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Fasting required for accurate measurement. Lowered by inflammation — check alongside CRP. Only ~0.1% of total body zinc is in serum. Long-term zinc >40mg/day can deplete copper.

biomarkers.name: Calcium (Serum)
biomarkers.description: Tightly regulated by parathyroid hormone, vitamin D, and calcitonin. Ionized calcium is more informative for true status.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Serum calcium is tightly regulated — can be 'normal' even with significant bone loss. Always check alongside albumin (correct for protein binding) and PTH.

biomarkers.name: HbA1c (Glycated Hemoglobin)
biomarkers.description: Reflects average blood glucose over the past 2-3 months. Gold standard for diabetes screening and monitoring.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Normal: <5.7%. Prediabetes: 5.7-6.4%. Diabetes: ≥6.5%. Functional practitioners target <5.0-5.2% for metabolic optimization.

biomarkers.name: Fasting Glucose
biomarkers.description: Blood sugar level after 8-12 hours of fasting. Screens for diabetes and metabolic dysfunction.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Normal: 70-99 mg/dL. Prediabetes: 100-125 mg/dL. Diabetes: ≥126 mg/dL. Functional optimal: 72-85 mg/dL.

biomarkers.name: Fasting Insulin
biomarkers.description: Measures baseline insulin production. Critical for detecting insulin resistance BEFORE glucose becomes abnormal.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Often NOT included in standard panels — must be specifically ordered. Critical for insulin resistance assessment. >6 µIU/mL suggests early insulin resistance even with normal glucose.

biomarkers.name: hs-CRP (High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein)
biomarkers.description: Highly sensitive marker of systemic inflammation. Predicts cardiovascular risk independent of cholesterol.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: AHA categories: <1.0 mg/L = low risk, 1.0-3.0 = average risk, >3.0 = high cardiovascular risk. >10.0 suggests acute infection (retest when resolved). Functional target: <0.7 mg/L.

biomarkers.name: Homocysteine
biomarkers.description: Amino acid byproduct of methylation. Elevated levels indicate methylation dysfunction and are linked to cardiovascular and neurological risk.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Elevated homocysteine is primarily addressed with B12, folate (5-MTHF), and B6 (P-5-P). MTHFR gene variants can impair methylation → elevated homocysteine.

biomarkers.name: TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone)
biomarkers.description: Primary screening test for thyroid function. Produced by pituitary gland to stimulate thyroid hormone production.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Standard range misses subclinical dysfunction. Functional medicine uses 1.0-2.0 mIU/L as optimal. Always test alongside Free T3, Free T4, and TPO antibodies for complete picture.

biomarkers.name: Free T3 (Triiodothyronine)
biomarkers.description: The most active thyroid hormone. Produced by conversion of T4 → T3 in peripheral tissues.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Low Free T3 with normal TSH may indicate poor T4→T3 conversion (check reverse T3). Conversion requires selenium, zinc, and iron.

biomarkers.name: Free T4 (Thyroxine)
biomarkers.description: Storage form of thyroid hormone. Converted to active T3 in peripheral tissues.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: T4 is the storage hormone produced directly by the thyroid. Low T4 + high TSH = primary hypothyroidism.

biomarkers.name: TPO Antibodies
biomarkers.description: Antibodies against thyroid peroxidase enzyme. Primary marker for autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's).
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Elevated in Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Selenium 200 mcg/day shown to reduce TPO antibodies in multiple studies. Gluten elimination may help some patients.

biomarkers.name: Total Cholesterol
biomarkers.description: Sum of HDL, LDL, and VLDL cholesterol. Context-dependent — less useful than LDL/HDL ratio or ApoB.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Desirable: <200 mg/dL. Borderline high: 200-239 mg/dL. High: ≥240 mg/dL. Context matters more than absolute number.

biomarkers.name: LDL Cholesterol
biomarkers.description: Primary atherogenic lipoprotein. 2026 AHA/ACC guidelines establish risk-based LDL-C targets.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Optimal: <100 mg/dL. Near optimal: 100-129. Borderline high: 130-159. High: 160-189. Very high: ≥190. ApoB may be more accurate.

biomarkers.name: HDL Cholesterol
biomarkers.description: Protective 'good' cholesterol that removes LDL from arteries. Higher is generally better.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Men: >40 mg/dL (optimal >55). Women: >50 mg/dL (optimal >65). Exercise, omega-3, and moderate alcohol increase HDL.

biomarkers.name: Triglycerides
biomarkers.description: Blood fats influenced by diet, especially refined carbs and alcohol. Fasting measurement preferred.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Normal: <150 mg/dL. Borderline: 150-199. High: 200-499. Very high: ≥500 (prescription omega-3 indicated per AHA). Functional optimal: <75-100 mg/dL.

high: Pancreatitis risk at very high levels

biomarkers.name: Omega-3 Index
biomarkers.description: Percentage of EPA+DHA in red blood cell membranes. Reflects long-term omega-3 status (past 120 days).
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: <4% = high cardiovascular risk zone. 4-8% = intermediate. ≥8% = optimal/cardioprotective. Most Americans fall <4%. Takes 3-4 months of supplementation to change.

biomarkers.name: Total Testosterone
biomarkers.description: Primary male sex hormone. Important for muscle mass, bone density, energy, mood, and libido in both sexes.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Evaluate alongside Free T and SHBG. Morning draw recommended (peak levels). Declining ~1%/year after age 30.

biomarkers.name: DHEA-S (Dehydroepiandrosterone Sulfate)
biomarkers.description: Adrenal precursor hormone. Marker of adrenal reserve and overall hormonal vitality. Declines significantly with age.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Peaks in 20s, declines ~2% per year. Low DHEA-S may indicate chronic stress/adrenal insufficiency. Age-dependent interpretation required.

biomarkers.name: Hemoglobin
biomarkers.description: Oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells. Primary measure for anemia.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Men: 13.5-17.5 g/dL. Women: 12-16 g/dL. Low hemoglobin = anemia (investigate cause: iron, B12, folate, chronic disease).

biomarkers.name: MCV (Mean Corpuscular Volume)
biomarkers.description: Average size of red blood cells. Helps classify type of anemia.
biomarkers.clinicalNotes: Low MCV (<80) = microcytic (iron deficiency). High MCV (>100) = macrocytic (B12/folate deficiency). Functional optimal 82-89.9 fL.
